title: Aloe vera cold-process soap
subtitle: A soothing bar with aloe vera juice replacing distilled water in the lye solution
excerpt: Replacing the water in a lye solution with aloe vera juice introduces the plant's polysaccharides and amino acids directly into the soap matrix. The sugars in aloe can accelerate trace, so this batch is made at a lower temperature than a standard cold-process bar.
sourceNotes: Aloe vera soap formulation drawing on Brambleberry and Soap Queen guides for milk and juice substitutions in cold-process soap.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 kg soap batter (~7 x 130 g bars after 4-week cure)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally and rerun through a lye calculator.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Minimum 4-week cure. The bar may be a creamy tan colour from the aloe sugars reacting with the lye.
recipe.temperatureNote: Combine oils and lye solution at room temperature (around 20 to 25 degrees Celsius) to prevent scorching the aloe sugars.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 2 years from cure date in cool, dry storage. Discard if rancid or showing DOS.

glossaryTerms.definition: Replacing all or part of the distilled water in a lye solution with a liquid that contains sugars, proteins, or other actives. Aloe vera juice, milk, and herbal teas are common choices. Sugary liquids can react with the lye to produce a darker colour and faster trace.
The percentage of oils left unsaponified in the finished soap, providing conditioning free fatty acids.
The emulsification point in cold-process soap making at which the batter holds a visible drizzle on its surface.

Unexpectedly fast thickening of a soap batter, caused by sugars, clays, or certain fragrance oils. In severe cases the batter becomes unpourable within minutes. Working at lower temperatures reduces the risk.
The 4 to 8 week resting period after unmoulding during which water evaporates and saponification completes.

content.text: Using aloe vera juice instead of plain water is a classic
in cold-process soap making. The aloe's natural sugars react with sodium hydroxide during the lye preparation phase and can cause

if the batter is made warm. Working at room temperature (20 to 25 degrees Celsius) and keeping the lye solution cold by starting with chilled or frozen aloe minimises this risk. The resulting bar has a
to a creamy, mild bar. Shelf life is 2 years from cure date.
technique before starting. Wear protective goggles and gloves throughout. Pour sodium hydroxide into the aloe juice, not the reverse. Use chilled or frozen aloe juice to control the heat spike.

items.prepNote: Confirm with a lye calculator; 5% superfat
Chilled or partially frozen; replaces distilled water in the lye solution

content.text: technique with one change: pour the aloe vera juice into your measuring jug and chill it, or use it partly frozen. Add sodium hydroxide slowly and stir throughout. The aloe turns orange-yellow and heats up quickly. Add the lye in small amounts to keep the temperature below 50 degrees Celsius.
Combine oils at room temperature. When the lye solution has cooled to roughly room temperature, pour it into the oils. Stick-blend to
in short bursts. The batter may thicken faster than a plain water batch. Pour at light trace, insulate, and leave 48 hours. Unmould, cut, and cure for 4 to 6 weeks.

Cured bars keep for 2 years in cool, dry storage. The bar may be a tan or honey colour from the aloe sugars reacting with the sodium hydroxide; this is normal and does not affect quality. Discard if the bar smells rancid or shows orange spots (DOS).
